expert_rationale: It must contain the question, selected skill, retrieved evidence, citations, reasoning summary, final decision, confidence, human-review flag, timestamp, policy versions, policy hashes, model or prompt version, and responsible owner.
Users and affected people should receive clear notice explaining the AI purpose, limitations, data categories, and how to request review.
External AI cloud use requires procurement approval, security assessment, data processing agreement, documented safeguards, and data-protection review where personal or sensitive data is involved.
Unsupported recommendations must be marked as uncertain, supported by citations where possible, and routed for review if evidence is missing or contradictory.
The stricter applicable rule applies, and unresolved conflicts must be escalated to the policy owner before operational use.
Trained human review is required before high-impact or adverse recommendations are implemented, especially for public services, eligibility, sensitive data, or vulnerable groups.
It is governance-ready only when the model identifier, model version, prompt or policy-skill version, configuration, timestamp, responsible owner, evidence, and policy hashes allow reconstruction.
Retrieval must respect user role, department scope, and document sensitivity; restricted or sensitive information must not be exposed outside approved access controls.
A low-risk administrative pilot may proceed only with no sensitive data leaving approved systems, documented monitoring, fallback procedures, user communication, and rollback criteria.
Non-anonymized citizen images require minimization, a documented legal basis, and data-protection review before any external processing.
Policy hashes allow later reconstruction of the exact policy evidence and version used for the decision, supporting traceability and audit review.
The current v2 pilot policy requires a responsible owner, policy-version logging, security assessment, data-protection review, rollback criteria, and review before use.
The current approved v2 policy applies; decisions must cite v2 and record the policy version and policy hash rather than relying on outdated v1 wording.
The system must be assessed for accessibility, language fairness, and disproportionate impact on protected or vulnerable groups before deployment.
It should identify the policy sources used, the responsible official, the decision status, and any unresolved uncertainty.
It should record the uncertainty, identify missing information, cite available evidence, and route the case to review when the uncertainty affects policy compliance.
Citations must correspond to retrieved policy evidence; mismatched or invented citations fail audit readiness and must be corrected or routed for review.
Recommendations must cite policy sections and clearly distinguish mandatory controls from optional good practice.
Personal data must be minimized, anonymized where possible, processed with a documented legal basis, and reviewed when sensitive or external processing is involved.
Only necessary personal data should be processed; anonymization should be used where possible, and sensitive or non-anonymized data requires review.
Fully automated adverse decisions about citizens are not allowed without contestability and meaningful human review.
AI systems must not manipulate vulnerable people; such use is prohibited and must not be authorized.
No. Test phases are not exempt from procurement approval, security assessment, data processing agreement, documented safeguards, and data-protection controls.
Rollback criteria ensure that a pilot can be stopped or reverted safely when policy, risk, or operational issues arise.
A named owner is required so accountability, review, rollback, and audit responsibilities are clear.
Policy-version logging records which policy version governed the decision so that future reviewers can reconstruct and verify the decision.
The reviewer must inspect evidence, override the system, and record a justification before implementation.
Audit records must be exportable for internal or external review and include the evidence, citations, decision, review flag, hashes, and timestamps needed for reconstruction.
It should avoid unsupported conclusions, mark uncertainty or missing evidence, and return needs review when a reliable policy decision cannot be made.
Confidence should be recorded with the decision, but low confidence or missing evidence should trigger uncertainty handling and review rather than unsupported approval.
A policy skill contains name, version, retrieval scope, required evidence tags, decision schema, human-review triggers, audit fields, failure policy, and prompt template.
The failure policy defines what the agent must do when required evidence, valid citations, or confidence are missing, usually escalating or requesting missing information.
Scoped retrieval limits evidence to relevant and authorized policy areas, improving grounding, access control, and auditability.
Human review is commonly triggered by high-risk public services, citizen images, sensitive data, external cloud use, adverse impacts, eligibility, conflicts, or missing mandatory controls.
Eligibility, access to public services, education, employment, law-enforcement support, and similar high-impact domains are treated as high-risk AI use.
Eligibility affects access to public services and is treated as high risk, requiring risk management, data governance, logging, transparency, and human oversight.
External AI cloud services may be used only after procurement approval and the related security, data-processing, and safeguard requirements are documented.
A data processing agreement is required to document responsibilities and safeguards before external AI cloud processing is used.
A security assessment verifies whether the external service and safeguards satisfy organizational security requirements before data or decisions are processed externally.
Systems should be assessed for accessibility, language fairness, and disproportionate impacts on protected or vulnerable groups before deployment.
Language fairness is relevant because public-sector systems must avoid disproportionate impacts and maintain accessibility for affected groups.
Access must respect user role, department scope, and document sensitivity, especially where restricted policy documents or sensitive data are involved.
The system should retrieve only policy evidence that the user is authorized to access within the relevant department and sensitivity scope.
Sensitive personal data may be sent externally only with explicit approval, documented safeguards, and data-protection review; otherwise it is not allowed.
The model identifier, model version, configuration, prompt version or policy-skill version, timestamp, and responsible owner must be recorded.
Recording the prompt or policy-skill version supports reproducibility and shows which governed decision logic was used.
Citations should prove that each recommendation is grounded in retrieved policy evidence and does not contradict the cited source.
The system should identify unresolved uncertainty, cite available policy sources, and route the case for review if uncertainty affects the decision.
The decision must cite current v2 pilot policy and policy-update evidence, including version logging and policy-hash requirements.
Unresolved precedence or policy conflicts should be escalated to the policy owner or responsible official before the action proceeds.
Yes, it is allowed or conditional as low-risk administrative support if monitoring, fallback, user communication, and audit records are documented.
No. Non-anonymized citizen images and external cloud processing require data-protection review and external-cloud controls; a test phase is not exempt.
No. External AI cloud services require procurement approval, security assessment, data processing agreement, and documented safeguards.
No. Fully automated adverse decisions about citizens are not allowed without contestability and meaningful human review.
Yes, it can proceed as conditional low-risk support when evidence citations, audit fields, and approved systems are used.
No. Current v2 requires a named owner, policy-version logging, security assessment, data-protection review, rollback criteria, and review before use.
Yes, it may proceed conditionally when all current v2 pilot controls and review requirements are documented.
No. Mandatory recommendations must cite policy sections; unsupported or uncited recommendations fail evidence-grounding requirements.
No. Sensitive personal data must not be sent externally unless explicit approval and documented safeguards exist.
No. Pilot deployments require rollback criteria or fallback procedures, especially under the current v2 policy.
No. Retrieval must respect user role, department scope, and document sensitivity.
It may proceed only conditionally as high-risk use with risk management, data governance, transparency, logging, and trained human oversight.
No. Inferring sensitive traits without a lawful basis is prohibited regardless of advisory framing.
No. Model governance and accountability require the responsible owner for auditability.
No. Audit trails require a timestamp so the decision can be reconstructed and reviewed.
No. Claims that contradict cited evidence must be flagged for review and cannot be accepted as compliant.
Yes, it can proceed conditionally as low-risk support if no sensitive personal data leaves approved systems and audit records are kept.
No. High-risk public-service AI requires transparency, including notice about purpose, limitations, data categories, and review rights.
No. High-impact or adverse recommendations require trained human review before implementation.
Yes, it may proceed conditionally if all external-cloud and pilot controls are documented and no sensitive data is involved.
No. When policy versions conflict, the current approved v2 policy applies and must be cited.
No. Missing required policy evidence means the decision must be marked uncertain or routed for review.
No. Recommendations must distinguish mandatory requirements from optional good practice.
Yes, it can proceed conditionally as low-risk administrative support with monitoring and auditability.
No. Health data is sensitive personal data and requires documented legal basis, safeguards, and data-protection review before external processing.
No. Public-sector systems must be assessed for accessibility, language fairness, and disproportionate impacts before deployment.
No. Governance-ready audit records must include policy versions and policy hashes.
No. The system must avoid unsupported conclusions and mark evidence as insufficient or route for review.
No. Current v2 does not allow pilots without a named responsible owner.
No. External cloud use also requires procurement approval, data processing agreement, and documented safeguards.
No. Employment-related AI is treated as high risk and requires documented controls and human oversight.
No. Accountability requires unresolved uncertainty to be identified and recorded.
No. The prompt version or policy-skill version must be recorded for reproducibility.
Yes, it can proceed conditionally if no sensitive data leaves approved systems and rollback criteria are documented.
No. Affected people should receive clear notice when AI materially supports a decision.
No. Citations must correspond to retrieved evidence and support the recommendation.
No. Policy conflicts require the stricter rule and escalation to the policy owner.
No. External cloud use still requires procurement approval, security assessment, DPA, and safeguards.
No. Retrieval must respect role, department scope, and document sensitivity.
No. The reviewer must be able to inspect evidence, override the system, and record justification.
No. A decision that cannot be reproduced from recorded evidence and configuration is not governance-ready.
No. Current v2 requires policy-version logging in addition to a named owner and other controls.
No. Accountability requires the decision status to be identified.
No. Education-related AI is high risk and requires risk management, logging, transparency, and human oversight.
Yes, it may proceed conditionally as evidence-grounded low-risk support.
No. Non-anonymized citizen images require data-protection review before external processing; temporary storage does not remove this requirement.
No. Human-review flags and routing rationale must be recorded in the audit trail.
No. Compliance requires decision validity, evidence grounding, review routing, and auditability, not only lexical overlap.
It may proceed only conditionally with data-protection review, documented safeguards, and trained human review.
Yes, it is conditionally compliant from an accountability perspective if the cited evidence supports the recommendation.
This is high-risk because it affects access to public services and requires risk management, logging, transparency, data governance, and human oversight.
This is low-risk administrative support and may proceed conditionally with monitoring, fallback, user communication, and auditability.
This is sensitive external processing and requires data-protection review and external-cloud controls; without them it is not allowed.
Employment-related AI is high risk and requires documented risk management, transparency, logging, and human oversight.
This is prohibited or not allowed because sensitive-trait inference requires a lawful basis and must not be performed unlawfully.
This is low to medium risk and may proceed conditionally if evidence citations, auditability, and approved systems are used.
This is high-risk public-service eligibility support and requires human oversight, logging, transparency, and data governance.
This is high governance risk because retrieval must respect user role, department scope, and document sensitivity.
This is not allowed under current v2 because pilots require a named responsible owner.
This is conditionally allowed but still governance-sensitive because external generative AI pilot use requires the documented v2 controls and review.
This is evidence-grounding risk and must be marked uncertain or routed for review.
This is prohibited because AI systems must not manipulate vulnerable people.
Education-related AI is high risk and requires risk management, transparency, logging, and human oversight.
This creates deployment and fairness risk; accessibility, language fairness, and disproportionate-impact assessment are required before deployment.
This is not allowed until the required data processing agreement and other external-cloud controls are documented.
This is low-risk administrative support and may proceed conditionally with monitoring and auditability.
This is auditability risk; the decision is not governance-ready without policy hashes for reconstruction.
This is not allowed because adverse or high-impact recommendations require trained human review before implementation.
This requires review and escalation; the stricter applicable rule must be applied.
This is not allowed because sensitive personal data must not be sent externally without explicit approval and documented safeguards.
This satisfies transparency expectations conditionally, assuming other applicable controls are also met.
This is compliance and evidence risk because mandatory requirements must be distinguished from optional good practice.
This is high evidence-faithfulness risk and must be routed for review.
Law-enforcement support is high risk and requires documented governance controls and human oversight.
This is low-risk or conditional if no sensitive personal data leaves approved systems and auditability is maintained.
This is not allowed under v2 because policy-version logging is required.
This is audit and evidence-grounding risk; invented citations fail validation and require correction or review.
This remains high risk because benefits data and eligibility decisions require risk controls, logging, transparency, and human oversight.

Under current v2 it is conditional only if owner, policy-version logging, security assessment, data-protection review, rollback criteria, and review are documented.
This is not governance-ready because AI-assisted decisions must produce an audit trail.
This may be low to medium risk for public documents, but role and sensitivity controls are still required if restricted documents can be retrieved.
This is not allowed because reviewers must be able to inspect evidence, override the system, and record justification.
This is high-risk AI and requires risk management, logging, transparency, and human oversight.
This creates accountability risk because the responsible official must be identified.
This is not allowed until procurement approval is completed with the other external-cloud controls.
This is low-risk administrative support if no sensitive personal data leaves approved systems and auditability is maintained.
This is not allowed under current-version governance because decisions must cite the current approved version.
This is high governance risk and requires fairness assessment, human oversight, and review before deployment.
This is more governance-ready because core audit-trail and reconstruction fields are recorded.
This is accountability risk because decision status must be identified.
This may be conditional if anonymization is effective, a legal basis exists, and processing stays within approved systems.
This is high risk and may be prohibited if it manipulates vulnerable people or lacks meaningful human review.
This is not allowed as a complete pilot deployment because fallback or rollback procedures are required.
This is not governance-ready because model identifier and model version must be recorded.
This is high risk and not allowed for adverse citizen decisions without contestability and meaningful human review.
This requires review because language fairness must be assessed before deployment.
This is evidence-grounding risk and should be routed for correction or review.
This is conditionally governance-ready if all v2 pilot controls are also satisfied.
This requires escalation to the policy owner and should not be authorized until precedence is resolved.
The current v2 requirements control; the stricter current rule applies and the case must be escalated or blocked until controls are documented.
The stricter external-cloud rule applies, so procurement approval, security assessment, DPA, and safeguards are required before proceeding.

The stricter pilot-deployment requirement applies and rollback criteria must be documented before the pilot proceeds.
The accessibility and fairness assessment should still be applied before public-facing deployment.
